mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Malawi on?
mapQuestions.answer: Africa, in the south-east. Malawi is landlocked, surrounded by Mozambique, Tanzania and Zambia 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Malawi?
mapQuestions.answer: Lilongwe, in the centre of the country. Blantyre, in the south, is the biggest commercial city.

mapQuestions.question: What languages does Malawi speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Chichewa is the most widely spoken African language; English is the other official language, used in schools, government and business.

mapQuestions.question: What covers a fifth of Malawi?
mapQuestions.answer: Lake Malawi! It is the third-largest lake in Africa, about 580 km long, and covers nearly 20% of the country.

mapQuestions.question: Why is Malawi called "The Warm Heart of Africa"?
mapQuestions.answer: Because Malawians are famous across the continent for being some of the friendliest, most welcoming people you can meet. The nickname has been around for decades.

foods.name: Nsima
foods.description: A firm white maize-flour porridge that is eaten with EVERY meal in Malawi. You break off a piece with your right hand, roll it into a ball, dip it in stew and eat. The Malawian staple.

foods.name: Chambo
foods.description: A type of large cichlid fish from Lake Malawi, usually grilled whole over charcoal and served with nsima and greens. The national fish.

foods.name: Mandasi
foods.description: Sweet fried doughnuts (similar to Kenya's mandazi), sold by the bagful at bus stands and dunked into milky tea. A Malawian breakfast.

foods.name: Thobwa
foods.description: A sweet, lightly fermented maize-and-millet drink, sold in plastic cups at markets. Looks like a thin porridge. Refreshing and totally non-alcoholic.

foods.name: Kachumbari
foods.description: A simple chopped salad of tomato, red onion, chilli and lime, eaten as a side with grilled fish and nsima. Sharp, fresh and brilliant.

foods.name: Chimanga wa kukazinga
foods.description: Roasted maize cobs on the street, blackened slightly over coals, eaten plain. The classic Malawian snack as you walk past a bus station.

scavengerItems.label: Lake Malawi (it stretches all the way to the horizon, like a sea)
A tea plantation (the green rolling fields around Mount Mulanje)
A child running out of a village waving "Bo!" or "Hello!" at the car
A "kabaza" bicycle taxi (an entire family taxi service on two wheels)
Mount Mulanje rising out of the tea fields like a giant island
A wooden dhow sailboat or fishing canoe on Lake Malawi
Dried fish (usacha) drying in the sun by the lake
A fish eagle calling above the lake (Malawi's national bird)
A massive baobab tree, standing alone on the plains
Hippos surfacing in the Shire River at Liwonde National Park
Elephants in Majete or Liwonde National Park (both bouncing back beautifully)
A village football match played with a ball made of plastic bags tied tight

geoMatches.description: The capital, in the centre of the country. Quiet, leafy, low-rise. The "Old Town" market is the busiest part.
The huge inland sea that defines the country. Crystal-clear, blue, and so big some Malawians have never reached the other side.
The "island in the sky" massif in the south, rising 3,000 m above the tea plantations. Long ridge hikes between mountain huts, with waterfalls and rare plants.
A small but spectacular national park along the Shire River. Hippos and elephants in huge numbers, and most of the Big Five.
A small island in the middle of Lake Malawi with white-sand beaches and a giant Anglican cathedral that looks like it has been picked up from England and dropped on the lake.
A high rolling grassland plateau in the north, more like Scotland than Africa. Wild zebras, antelopes and 200+ orchid species.

senses.smell: Woodsmoke from cooking fires at dusk, fresh tea leaves drying near Mulanje, the lake on a damp morning, ripe mangoes in season, hot tarmac on a sunny afternoon…
senses.hear: Chichewa greetings everywhere ("Moni!" "Zikomo!"), the call of the fish eagle over the lake, village football matches with shouts of "Goal!", a wooden dugout paddle dipping in the lake, kabaza taxi bells…
senses.taste: Smoky grilled chambo straight from the lake, warm soft nsima with stew, sweet mandasi dunked into milky tea, sharp lime kachumbari, sweet ripe Malawian mangoes…
senses.feel: Soft Malawi lake water (some of the warmest swimming water in Africa), tea leaves between your fingers, the cool grass of Nyika Plateau, a bumpy dirt road, the gentle press of crowds at a market…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How big is Lake Malawi, really?
stories.body: Lake Malawi is so big that early European explorers thought they had reached the sea. It is 580 km long, up to 80 km wide, and 700 m deep. It is the third-largest lake in Africa (after Lake Victoria and Lake Tanganyika) and one of the deepest lakes in the world. It covers nearly 20% of Malawi itself. The lake is incredibly clear, sandy-bottomed in many places, and warm enough to swim in all year. The Shire River drains out the southern end of the lake and flows eventually into the Zambezi and the Indian Ocean.

stories.question: Why is Malawi called "the warm heart of Africa"?
stories.body: The nickname has been around for decades, and almost every traveller who comes to Malawi confirms it. Greetings here are long and important: you ask "Muli bwanji?", you wait for the answer, then they ask back, and only after that do you get to whatever you were actually going to talk about. People come out of their houses to wave at passing cars. Children chase the car shouting "Bo! Bo!" (a greeting that means "what's up?"). Strangers walking the same road in the same direction become walking companions within minutes. Malawi is one of the poorer countries in the world, but its reputation for friendliness is one of the warmest on the continent.

stories.question: How many fish species live in this one lake?
stories.body: Lake Malawi is home to MORE fish species than any other lake on Earth, around 1,000 species (and counting, more are still being discovered). Most of them are "cichlids", small brightly coloured fish a bit like freshwater tropical-aquarium fish. The cichlids of Lake Malawi all evolved here, from a few common ancestors, in just the last few hundred thousand years, into a stunning rainbow of colours and shapes. Every island has its own cichlid varieties that exist nowhere else. The pet trade for tropical fish around the world pulls many of its most beautiful aquarium species directly from Lake Malawi.

stories.question: How did Lake Malawi first appear on European maps?
stories.body: In September 1859, the Scottish explorer and missionary David Livingstone became the first European to see Lake Malawi (which he called "Lake Nyasa"). He was travelling up the Shire River from the Zambezi with a small expedition. Livingstone is a complicated figure: he was a real opponent of the Atlantic slave trade, but he also opened up "the interior" to European colonisation that followed afterwards. His route along the lake later became central to Malawi's history, including the founding of the city of Blantyre (named after Livingstone's birthplace in Scotland). Today Livingstone is remembered by many places in Malawi and Zambia carrying his name.

stories.question: Did Mount Mulanje inspire The Hobbit?
stories.body: It is one of those great unprovable rumours. The novelist J.R.R. Tolkien (who wrote "The Lord of the Rings" and "The Hobbit") was born in South Africa and never visited Malawi, BUT his close friend and family member Laurens van der Post lived in Malawi and wrote books about Mount Mulanje. Some Tolkien researchers believe that Mulanje, a flat-topped massif rising abruptly out of green tea plains, may have inspired some of the misty mountains of Middle Earth. Whether it really did or not, climbing Mulanje feels like climbing into a fantasy world: clouds pour over the ridges, waterfalls cut down the sides, and you sleep in stone mountain huts each night.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A place we have never seen can still inspire a story we love. What real-life landscape do you imagine when you read your favourite book?

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Malawi so far?
Have you tried saying "moni" and "zikomo" to anyone? How was the response?
Lake, mountain, plateau, capital, which has felt the most "Malawi"?
Have we noticed how long Malawian hellos are? How does that feel?
If we had to pick one place to come back to in Malawi, which would it be?
Lake Malawi has more fish species than any lake on Earth. Why might so many evolve in one place?

wordSearch.hint: Malawian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Lake Malawi cichlid
animals.description: Brightly coloured small fish, with over 1,000 species in this one lake. Snorkel along the rocky shores to see them.

animals.name: African fish eagle
animals.description: The national bird of Malawi. Its loud yelping call is the sound of the lake.

animals.name: Hippopotamus
animals.description: In Liwonde National Park along the Shire River, often dozens visible from the riverbank at dusk.

animals.name: African elephant
animals.description: Liwonde and Majete national parks both have growing herds.

animals.name: Nile crocodile
animals.description: In the Shire River and along Lake Malawi shores. Always check with locals before swimming.

animals.name: Plains zebra
animals.description: On the Nyika Plateau and in Majete, often grazing alongside antelopes.

animals.name: Leopard
animals.description: Returning to Liwonde and Majete after years of being almost wiped out. Hard to spot but worth trying.
